revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Hitsujisaki-jinja (Minato, Ishinomaki)]][[ja:零羊崎神社 (石巻市湊)]] {{Expand Japanese|零羊崎神社 (石巻市湊)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Hitsujisaki Shrine | native_name = 零羊崎神社 (石巻市湊) | image = Hitsujisaki jinja (Minato) Haiden.JPG | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Hitsujisaki Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|38.439854|141.341651}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi|az|Vatatsumi|de|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|es|Watatsumi|fa|واتاتسومی|fr|Watatsumi|hu|Vatacumi|ja|ワタツミ|ko|와타쓰미|mni|ꯋꯇꯠꯁꯨꯃꯤ|pl|Watatsumi|ru|Ватацуми|simple|Watatsumi|uk|Вататсумі|zh|綿津見|qid=Q1474187|1=Watatsumi}} }} {{nihongo|'''Hitsujisaki Shrine'''|零羊崎神社 (石巻市湊)}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Mutsu Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Myojin Taisha|lt=Myōjin Taisha|en|Myojin Taisha|fr|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|simple|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}} * {{ill|Q134986946|ltq=Q134986946|qid=Q134986946|1=Q134986946}} * Province|ja|陸奥国の式内社一覧|qid=Q11658590|1=List of Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province}} ** Series ordinal: 43 ** P155: {{ill|Q11616108|ltq=Q11616108|qid=Q11616108|1=Kusano Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Katoriitsuno- Shrine|lt=Katoriitsuno- Shrine|qid=Q135040002|1=Katoriitsuno- Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q270814|ltq=Q270814|qid=Q270814|1=Ishinomaki}} * {{ill|Q907389|ltq=Q907389|qid=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} * {{ill|Oshika District, Miyagi|lt=Oshika district|ceb|Oshika Gun|en|Oshika District, Miyagi|fr|District d'Oshika|id|Distrik Oshika, Miyagi|it|Distretto di Oshika|ja|牡鹿郡|ko|오시카군|pl|Powiat Oshika|ru|Осика (уезд)|sr|Област Ошика|uk|Повіт Осіка|zh_yue|牡鹿郡|zh|牡鹿郡|qid=Q1131921|1=Oshika District, Miyagi}} == coordinate location (P625) == (P1343) == * {{ill|Kokugakuin University Shrine database (old)|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database (old)|qid=Q135037878|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database (old)}} ** P2699: https://web.archive.org/web/20151121015607/http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp:80/db/jinja/271603.html == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 182080 ** P3831: ᥓᥣᥱ ᥙᥣᥢᥱ|tet|Japaun|te|జపాన్|tg|Ҷопон|th|ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|thnews|หมวดหมู่:ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|tig|ጃፓን|ti|ጃፓን|tk|Ýaponiýa|tl|Hapon|tly|Japonijə|tn|Japan|tok|ma Nijon|to|Siapani|tpi|Siapan|trv|Tanah Tunux|tr|Japonya|trnews|Kategori:Japonya|trquote|Japonya|trvoyage|Japonya|ts|Japani|tt|Япония|tum|Japan|tw|Japan|tyv|Япония|ty|Tāpōnē|udm|Япония|ug|ياپونىيە|uk|Японія|uknews|Категорія:Японія|ukquote|Японія|ukvoyage|Японія|ur|جاپان|uz|Yaponiya|vec|Japon|vep|Japonii|ve|Japan|vi|Nhật Bản|vibooks|Các quốc gia/Nhật Bản|vivoyage|Nhật Bản|vls|Japan|vo|Yapän|war|Hapon|wa|Djapon|wo|Sapoŋ|wuu|日本|xal|Ниxуудин Нутг|xh|IJapani|xmf|იაპონია|yi|יאפאן|yo|Japan|za|Nyiedbonj|zea|Japan|zgh|ⵏⵉⴱⵓⵏ|zh_classical|日本|zh_min_nan|Ji̍t-pún|zh_yue|日本|zh|日本|zhnews|Category:日本|zhquote|日本|zhvoyage|日本|zu|IJaphani|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * ひつじさきじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 8370305000353 == Commons category (P373) == * Hitsujisaki-jinja (Minato, Ishinomaki) == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Hitsushisakino Shrine|lt=Hitsushisakino Shrine|qid=Q135040001|1=Hitsushisakino Shrine}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '宮城県石巻市湊字牧山7', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '宮城県石巻市湊', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Hitsujisaki-jinja (Minato, Ishinomaki)}} [[JA:零羊崎神社 (石巻市湊)]] {{wikidata link|Q11660144}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Reiyazaki Shrine |Image = [[File:Hitsujisaki jinja (Minato) Haiden.JPG|270px|Reiyazaki Shrine Hall of Worship]]
Shrine of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|qid=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ishinomaki City|Q270814|qid=Q270814|1=Ishinomaki}}Minato Aza Makiyama 7 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 steps degrees minutes seconds|38|26|22.7|N|141|20|29.7|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Zerohitsuzaki Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|qid=Q1474187|lt=Toyotamahiko no Mikoto|1=Watatsumi}} |Shakaku = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|lt=Meishindai|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Foundation = 15th {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|qid=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}2 |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternative name = Makiyama Shrine, Makiyama Omiya, Makiyama Reihitsuzaki Shrine |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=May 8th|Q2555|qid=Q2555|1=May 8}} (Regular Festival) |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Miyagi|label=Reiyazaki Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Reiyoshizaki Shrine'' (Hitsujisaki Jinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|Q47896|qid=Q47896|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ishinomaki City|Q270814|qid=Q270814|1=Ishinomaki}} Minato.{{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) {{ill|lt=Ronsha|Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}, {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is located on the left bank of the {{ill|lt=Kitakami River|Q1348297|qid=Q1348297|1=Kitakami River}}, on the top of {{ill|lt=Makiyama (Miyagi Prefecture)|Q136716676|qid=Q136716676|lt=Makiyama|1=Makiyama}}, approximately 250 meters above sea level. === Enshrined God === * '''{{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|qid=Q1474187|lt=Toyotamahiko no Mikoto (Owatatsumi no Kami)|1=Watatsumi}}''' - Known as the god of maritime protection === History === === Founding === The year of construction is unknown. {{ill|lt=Yasunaga (era name)|Q1193910|qid=Q1193910|lt=Yasunaga|1=An'ei}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1772|Q7690|qid=Q7690|1=1772}}) "{{ill|lt=Hunai Fudoki|Q11458377|qid=Q11458377|1=Hunai Fudoki}} Volume 13" Included in "Hunai Fudoki Volume 2" in 1975. states that it was enshrined on Minato-mura Tatsumakijima (present-day Makiyama) from the Saigoku region by the edict of {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|qid=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}. According to the history book, in the second year of Emperor Ojin's reign, at the imperial request of Empress Jingu, he was given the divine name ``Himitsu ni Sake no Kami,'' and was enshrined at Mt. Tatsumaki, Oshika District, as the god of protection for the eastern depths. The shrine's name, ``Hitsujisaki,'' comes from the deity of Mangeibetsu, and Tatsumakiyama came to be called ``Makiyama'' after the character for dragon was removed. === Discussion over Meishin Taisha ratio === Our company was designated as the Meishin Taisha "Reiyazaki Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. "Reihitsuzaki Shrine" is "{{ill|lt=Japan Three Generations True Story|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}" {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|qid=Q23897|1=859}}) January 27th article states, ``The advancement and new ordination of the seven gods of Gyeonggi. One of the 267 shrines is {{ill|lt=Junior Rank|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}. }In the article of January 27, 859, in the ``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'', there is an article that says, ``Shogoi 5th rank Gekuno 4th rank Shibahiko no kami. 4th rank of Shiwa Himegami, junior rank of 4th rank, lower rank," so it seems that his divine rank before promotion was Seigo 5th rank, lower rank of 6th grade, but it is also possible that he was promoted from 6th rank of unranked prince. It is recorded that he was promoted to . Also, {{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=extended|1=Enchō}} in the 5th year ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}) in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}", it is ranked as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}, and also "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" In Volume 3, it is listed as a target shrine for the Meishin Festival. However, the name of Reihitsuzaki Shrine no longer appears in historical materials after that, and controversy arose during the Edo period."Ishinomaki City History Volume 2" From "Ishinomaki City History Volume 2" 1956. According to , our company received a sacred golden dragon from {{ill|lt=Kiyohira Fujiwara|Q5360232|qid=Q5360232|1=Fujiwara no Kiyohira}}, a silver dragon and a divine sword from {{ill|lt=Kiyoshige Kasai|Q11621006|qid=Q11621006|1=Kasai Kiyoshige}}, and {{ill|lt=long sword|Q120676|qid=Q120676|1=Tachi}} from Kiyotsune Kasai and Kiyomune Kasai, which shows that the lords were highly revered for generations in the Middle Ages. Furthermore, in the first year of {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1661|Q7005|qid=Q7005|1=1661}}), the {{ill|lt=Kuroinjo|Q11678035|qid=Q11678035|1=kokuinjō}} of the {{ill|lt=Date clan|Q1151061|qid=Q1151061|lt=Date family|1=Date clan}} received a donation of 5 kanmon from the shrine. Later, in the middle of the Edo period, the theory that the shrine was Reihitsuzaki Shrine began to be advocated.{{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|qid=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1719|Q6902|qid=Q6902|1=1719}}) "Ou Kanseimon Roshi Volume 9" Included in "Sendai Series Volume 15 Ou Kanseimon Roshi Vol. 1" 1972. identified Makiyama as the ancient Reihitsuzaki Shrine. According to the article on Reihitsuzaki Shrine in the same book, it is one of the places where the Buddhist monk Shisho stole the original name of the shrine and changed the place name, causing the historic site to disappear. He also touches on the state of the shrine shrine at the time, and notes that there was a festival held on the 8th of April and September every year, and people from nearby areas gathered there, and a market was set up at the festival and business was active, but people thought it was a festival of Kannon, even though they set up a mikoshi and offered heihaku. Makiyama Kannon is described in the article on Makiyama Daihikaku in the same book. According to this, the name of the temple was Washiminezan Chozenji, the principal image was a secret Buddha that was said to have been pulled up from the ocean floor, and in front of the principal object was a statue said to be the work of Keishin. It is said that it was founded by {{ill|lt=Jien|Q715993|qid=Q715993|lt=Jishin|1=Jien}}, or that it is said to be one of the three great temples built by {{ill|lt=Sakanoue Tamuramaro|Q941979|qid=Q941979|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} along with Minmine and Toyama.{{ill|lt=Kanpo|Q1145798|qid=Q1145798|1=Kanpō}} 1st year ({{ill|lt=1741|Q7573|qid=Q7573|1=1741}}) "Honai Meisekishi Volume 14" Included in "Sendai Series Volume 8" in 1972. also introduces Reihitsuzaki Shrine and Makiyama Daihikaku in a similar manner to ``Ou Kansekimonroshi,'' but it also notes that Reihitsuzaki Shrine no longer has a shrine, only a group of shrines, and that the annual festival held in April and September of each year is called the Sajo Festival. In addition, the name of Makiyama Daihikaku was changed to Washimineyama Chozen-ji Temple, and along with Minmine and Toyama Hiikaku, the people of the area were known as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tamura Sankannon|1=UNKNOWN}}'Japanese Gods. -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 12 Tohoku/Hokkaido'' (1984), there are three Tamura Kannon temples: Makiyama (our company), Mitake (currently Mitake, Wakuya-cho, Toda-gun), and Toyama (currently Tetaru, Matsushima-cho, Miyagi-gun). It is written that it says.``Hunai Fudoki Volume 13'' written in 1772 provides the most detailed information about Reihitsuzaki Shrine and Makiyama Daihikaku. First, it introduces that Reihitsuzaki Shrine is currently called Hakusan Shrine and enshrines Toyotamahiko no Mikoto, that it was the first shrine in Oshika District and was once a large shrine, and that it is the guardian of the Minato Shichigo. Furthermore, as mentioned at the beginning, the company explains the founding of the company, and explains the history of each of the many subsidiary shrines outside the Yamauchi mountains, which are said to be a family tradition of the {{ill|lt=yamabushi|Q2309838|qid=Q2309838|lt=shugenja|1=Yamabushi}} Fumyoin family, whose ancestors were the priests of Reihitsuzaki Shrine. For these reasons, the Fumyoin family held Shinto rituals and offered mikoshi (portable shrines), but from the Shinto ritual in {{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|qid=Q1145262|1=Hōei}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1706|Q6835|qid=Q6835|1=1706}}), the Enzan Hoin of Makiyama Kannon's {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|qid=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}}, Chozenji Temple, was The Fumyoin family was excluded from the dedication, and from {{ill|lt=Horeki|Q1194397|qid=Q1194397|1=Hōreki}}10 ({{ill|lt=1760|Q7646|qid=Q7646|1=1760}}), Chozen-ji Temple, which commanded the village chief, eliminated all the old customs of the Shinto rituals that had been administered by the Fumyoin family, and began to call them Hakusan Shinto rituals instead of Reiyazaki Shrine rituals. ``Honai Fudoki Continuing in Volume 13, Chozenji was founded by {{ill|lt=Ennin|Q732282|qid=Q732282|lt=Jikaku Daishi|1=Ennin}} in the {{ill|lt=Kasho|Q1373157|qid=Q1373157|lt=Kasho year|1=Kashō}} to create the sacred body of Hakusan Gongen. Because of this, it is written in ``Ou Kansekimon Roshi'' and ``Hunai Meisekishi'' that there is no shrine of Reihitsuzaki Shrine now, only a group of shrines, and it is also called Hakusan Shrine. Also, in the description of Makiyama Daihikaku in the same book, in {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|qid=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 17th year ({{ill|lt=798|Q33460|qid=Q33460|1=798}}) Sakanoue Tamuramaro visited Mitake and Toyama Kannon. Local people say that it is one of the three Tamura Kannon temples that were built at the same time, but it is said that the land used to be the site of the old Reihitsuzaki Shrine, and that a Buddhist monk stole the land and hid the name of the Reihitsuzaki Shrine and called it Makiyama Kannon. About the Bettochochozen-ji Temple, "Honai Fudoki" Volume 13 is a {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|qid=Q668188|1=Tiantai}} temple of the {{ill|lt=Musashikuni|Q907398|qid=Q907398|1=Musashi Province}}{{ill|lt=Kaneiji|Q3120766|qid=Q3120766|lt=Higashieizan|1=Kan'ei-ji}} branch temple. It is introduced that {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Enchin|1=UNKNOWN}} founded the temple in the Kasho era, and Jikaku Daishi {{ill|lt=chuko|Q10871860|qid=Q10871860|1=chuko}} renamed it Makiyama-dera for the first time, and then renamed it Chozen-ji when Eizon Hoin was revived in the {{ill|lt=Manji|Q1145295|qid=Q1145295|lt=Manji years|1=Manji (era)}}. The founder of this revitalization, the Eizon Hoin, is enshrined at the Eizon Shrine within our company's grounds.In addition to these, in the article on Mano Village in ``Hunai Fudoki Volume 13'', it is written that there is Reihitsuzaki Shrine in Mano Village as well. According to this, when the mikoshi that had been at Reihitsuzaki Shrine in Mano was moved to Makiyama, the shrine's deity name was also taken away by Makiyama due to a plot by a Buddhist monk. It is written in the ``Inai Town History'' that there was a dispute between Reihitsuzaki Shrine in Mano Village and our company. According to this, Kanjuin of Mano gave a vigorous lecture to the Fumeiin family's claim that Hakusangu Shrine in Makiyama was Reihitsuzaki Shrine, and the dispute was brought to the domain office. {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|qid=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=1768|Q7675|qid=Q7675|1=1768}}) In response to a summons, the priest of Kanju-in appeared at the Sendai Hyojosho on March 3 and was questioned regarding the relationship between Fumyō-in, Makiyama Hakusangu Shrine, and Mano-Rehitsuzaki Shrine. In response, Kanju-in claimed that the mikoshi, which had been kept there for a long time, was temporarily entrusted to Makiyama Hakusangu Shrine because Mano Village had experienced poor harvests over the years, making it difficult to repair the shrine, and that the mikoshi did not originally belong to Makiyama. The evaluation office recognized this and handed down a decision to enshrine the Reihitsuzaki Shrine as the guardian deity of Mano Village, saying that Kanjuin's claims had been successfully heard. "Inai Town History" 1960. . === After Meiji === In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, Makiyama Kannon was restored by the chief priest in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}3 ({{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|qid=Q7741|1=1870}}) due to the {{ill|lt=separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|qid=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} and came to be called ``Reiyazaki Shrine.'' The main hall was used as a worship hall and the main shrine, and the principal image was moved to Baikei-ji Temple, a branch of Chozen-ji Temple, but it was later destroyed in a fire "Japanese Gods - Shrines and Sacred Sites - 12 Tohoku and Hokkaido" 1984. . In the 7th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|qid=Q7761|1=1874}}), it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and was later designated as a shrine dedicated to divine food. The current main hall was newly built in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}3 ({{ill|lt=1928|Q19715|qid=Q19715|1=1928}}), and the old main hall, which had been used for multiple purposes until then, was used as a worship hall. In 1942 ({{ill|lt=1942|Q18625|qid=Q18625|1=1942}}) it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}, and in 1952 ({{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|qid=Q5272|1=1952}}) a new stone Myojin torii gate was built. {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}In the {{ill|lt=Great East Japan Earthquake|Q1136168|qid=Q1136168|1=Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}} in March 2011 ({{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|qid=Q1994|1=2011}}), the tsunami reached the foot of Makiyama, where our company is located, and some of the nearby residents evacuated to our company on higher ground. For this reason, the area in front of the main building of Eizon Shrine, a shrine within the precincts, became a temporary evacuation center. === Precincts === === Shrine building === Shrine building (haiden, hall of worship, main hall) *The worship hall was the main hall of Washimineyama Chozenji Temple during the mixed Shinto and Buddhist era, and was renovated in 1933. The shrine, the Ikkensha style main shrine, and Mizugaki were rebuilt from 1938 to 1944. On the right side of the shrine is a stone pagoda built in the 7th year of Bunka ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|qid=Q6943|1=1814}}). File:Honden of Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki 2.JPG|Honden of Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine
Constructed in 1928. File:Haiden of Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki 2.JPG|Haden of worship File:Hitsujisaki jinja Sourintou.JPG|Hitsujisaki jinja Sourintou
The stone pagoda of Washimineyama Chozenji Temple that existed in this area before the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. Built in 1814, it is a cultural property designated by Ishinomaki City. '''Inside the temple''' Above the entrance to the shrine, there is a plaque reading ``Washiminezan Chozen-ji Temple,'' and inside the shrine are dedicated ``Diagrams of a large white horse'' and ``Diagrams of a large votive black horse.'' The Washiminezan Chozenji plaque and two large votive tablets are designated cultural assets by Ishinomaki City. File:Inside of Haiden of Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki 1.JPG|Inside of HaidenFile:Inside of Heiden of Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki 1.JPG|Heiden File:Explanation board of "Shuuhousan Chouzenji-Temple".JPG|Mount Washimine Chozenji Temple plaque at the entrance to the shrine of worship File:Big Ema board of White horse of Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki.JPG|Big Ema "Illustration of White Horse" File:Big Ema board of Black horse of Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki.JPG|Big Ema "Drawing of Black Horse" === Others === The first torii gate is located near the Daimonzaki pedestrian bridge on {{ill|lt=National Route 398|Q6157441|qid=Q6157441|1=Japan National Route 398}} on the south side of Makiyama. From there, the approach continues to Reihitsuzaki Shrine at the summit of Mt. Makiyama. File:Precincts of Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki.JPG|Precincts File:Torii gate of Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki.JPG|Torii gate in front of the shrine File:Hitsujisaki jinja (Minato) Gate.JPG|Torii
A stone Myojin torii gate built in 1952. File:Hitsujisaki jinja (Minato) Big gate.JPG|Otorii
Otorii stands on National Route 398. On the right is a stone marker that says ``Prefectural Shrine Reihitsuzaki Shrine.'' File:Hitsujisaki jinja Gate after Tsunami.JPG|Otorii immediately after the Great Earthquake === Setsusha === Miyoshi Shrine, Doso Shrine, and Kofugi Inari Shrine are located along the unpaved approach to Eizon Shrine from the west side of the shrine building. * Hakusan Yakushi Shrine *: A small shrine in front of the shrine. * Miyoshi Shrine *: Miyoshi Okami and 10 other deities are enshrined together. This shrine was founded by {{ill|lt=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine|Q11443949|qid=Q11443949|1=Taiheizan Miyoshi Shrine}} in Akita Prefecture, and is revered as the god of bountiful harvests, maritime safety, large catches, and physical strength. * Doso Shrine *: Dedicates the god Sarutahiko. Many wooden phallic-shaped shrines and stone shrines carved with phalluses are dedicated there. * Kofugi Inari Shrine * Eizon Shrine *: Katagiri Eizon (Eizon Hoin) is enshrined here. Eizon Hoin became a chief of staff at Chozen-ji Temple and was a very famous person because he devoted himself to the development and promotion of Ishinomaki. Eizohoin was exiled to Enoshima, Oshika District, for the rest of his life as a result of an innocent crime being fabricated by Shinemon Sasamachi, a landowner jealous of his fame. After Eizon Hoin was exiled, a fire broke out in Ishinomaki, and after Eizon Hoin passed away, Sasamachi Shinemon went insane and a series of strange events occurred, such as killing his family members. Fifty years after his death, Eizon Hoin was exonerated by the domain, and Eizon Shrine was built by villagers who admired Hoin's virtues. He is worshiped as a ``god who helps the weak,'' and is also revered as a ``god who protects justice,'' ``prevents fires,'' and ``protects safe childbirth.'' File:Hakusan-Yakushi-jinja shrine,Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Ishinomaki.JPG|Hakusan-Yakushi-jinja shrineFile:Torii gate of Sankichi-jinja shrine,Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki.JPG|Torii gate of Sankichi Shrine File:Sankichi-jinja shrine,Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki.JPG|Sankichi Shrine shrine building File:Dousojin shrine of Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki.JPG|Dousojin shrine File:Stone shrine of Doso-jinja shrine 2.JPG|Stone shrine of Doso-jinja shrine File:Fubugi inari-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki.JPG|Fubuki Inari Shrine File:Eison-jinja shrine,Hitsujisaki-jinja shrine,Minato,Ishinomaki.JPG|Eison Shrine Worship Hall *In addition, in front of the shrine building, there is a ritual place where prayers are accepted, amulets and talismans are given, and goshuin stamps are recorded.If you follow the road heading west, you will find Eizon Shrine, the shrine office, and a shrine garden. === Local information === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Reihitsuzaki Shrine (Mano, Ishinomaki City)|Q11660145|qid=Q11660145|1=Hitsujisaki Shrine (Mano, Ishinomaki)}} - A shrine with the same name in Ishinomaki City. It is also said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisharonsha|1=Shikinaisha}}. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=demon woman|1=UNKNOWN}} === External link === {{Commons|Category:Hitsujisaki-jinja (Minato, Ishinomaki)}} * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010071 Reihitsuzaki Shrine] (Miyagi Prefectural Shrine Agency) * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/271603.html Reiyousaki Shrine] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection”) {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Hitsujisaki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Hitsujisaki Shrine]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:石巻市の歴史]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:三吉神社]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:奥州七観音]]
